cataloging_subject.label: The Legend of Theophilus (Rawlinson tail-rhyme version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The single anonymous Middle English tail-rhyme version of the Theophilus legend preserved at ff. 11-15 of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. poet. 225 and represented by CME00082. It is not the complete manuscript, the other Middle English Theophilus versions, Wilhelm Heuser's complete 1903 German article, either scanned carrier volume, the U-M encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Legend of Theophilus (Rawlinson tail-rhyme version)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Rawlinson poet
catalog_summary.date: Composition not securely dated; sole surviving witness copied in the second half of the fifteenth century; first published in 1903
catalog_summary.region: Scribal language localized to Ely by LALME/eLALME; place of composition unknown

titles.value: The Legend of Theophilus (Rawlinson tail-rhyme version)
titles.supplied_by: General Corpus, following the established Theophilus identification and Beverly Boyd's Rawlinson-version title while distinguishing this text from the Northern Homily Cycle and South English Legendary versions

titles.value: Listeneth both great and small / I will you tellen a little tale
notes: Heuser's transcription begins 'Listenyth bothe, grete & smale, / I wil ȝow tellen a litel tale'.

titles.value: Hic incipit historia quomodo Tiofelus vendidit animam suam diabolo & quomodo per sanctam mariam deliberatus fuit
titles.supplied_by: DIMEV record 3109, identified there as the manuscript title at f. 11

assertion.notes: The manuscript image is not public, and Heuser prints 'liberatus' rather than DIMEV's 'deliberatus'.

titles.value: Hic incipit historia quomodo Tiofelus vendidit animam suam diabolo et quomodo per sanctam mariam liberatus fuit
titles.supplied_by: Wilhelm Heuser's 1903 transcription of the manuscript rubric
DIMEV record 3109 and Heuser's transcription, located at f. 15

titles.value: Eine neue mittelenglische Version der Theophilus-Sage

scope.description: Edition-layer title for the article that introduces and prints the poem, not a medieval work title

agents.name: Anonymous Rawlinson poet
notes: A conventional label for the unidentified author or adapter of this version; it does not identify the scribe of MS. Rawl. poet. 225.

scope.description: The 1903 first edition represented by CME00082
Heuser's complete transcription of the poem from MS. Rawl. poet. 225, ff. 11-15
German introduction and Bemerkungen retained around the poem in CME00082 but excluded from the abstract medieval work

date_statements.display: Not securely established; the sole witness is from 1450-1500, while Heuser suggests that the poem may preserve an older original

assertion.notes: No normalized composition range is supplied because the witness date is not automatically the work's date.

date_statements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century, in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. poet. 225

assertion.notes: The date belongs to the sole surviving witness and is not a normalized composition date.
Heuser presents the previously unknown manuscript text, Boyd calls him the first editor, and DIMEV lists no earlier edition.
DIMEV reports LALME/eLALME Linguistic Profile 73, grid 548 300, localized to Ely. This is a localization of scribal language, not proof that the poem was composed there.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: German introduction and commentary, French comparison lines, and the Latin witness rubric are edition or paratext layers and are not whole-work languages.

scope.description: The quoted acclamation 'Te deum laudamus' encoded as the seventh L element in stanza group 106

form_statements.description: A tail-rhyme poem of 107 normally six-line stanzas with rhyme pattern aabccb; stanza 106 also quotes the one-line Latin acclamation 'Te deum laudamus'.

assertion.notes: Boyd argues that the poem's dialogue and narrative conventions are better explained by tail-rhyme romance than by a direct miracle-play source.

subject_statements.label: Clerical ambition and a pact with the devil
Anti-Jewish sorcerer stereotype
notes: The tale portrays an unnamed Jewish intermediary as the agent who leads Theophilus to Satan. This heading records harmful anti-Jewish narrative content rather than endorsing it.

summaries.value: Theophilus, an archdeacon proud of his wealth and rank, suddenly loses his prosperity. An unnamed Jewish intermediary offers to restore him and leads him to Satan, whom Theophilus serves after renouncing Christ and Mary and signing a charter in his own blood. His rank and wealth restored, Theophilus later hears the heavenly command 'Revertere', repents, and appeals to the Virgin. Mary descends to hell, overcomes Satan, retrieves the charter, and returns it to Theophilus, who closes with praise of her miracles. The poem reshapes a Marian miracle as a compact tail-rhyme narrative and contains a harmful anti-Jewish sorcerer stereotype. CME00082 retains Heuser's German introduction and editorial comments around the complete Middle English poem, but those modern frames are not part of the cataloged medieval work.

The poem division has 107 LG elements and 643 L elements. DIMEV describes 107 six-line stanzas; the extra encoded line is the Latin quotation 'Te deum laudamus' inside LG 106. A raw count of all 649 XML L elements also includes six French or Middle English comparison lines in Heuser's German preface, so it is not the poem's line count.

DIMEV identifies only MS. Rawl. poet. 225, ff. 11-15, for this version and distinguishes it from the Northern Homily Cycle and South English Legendary versions. Heuser rejects Rutebeuf's play as a direct exemplar and leaves the poem's more precise source relationship unresolved; Boyd instead explains its treatment through tail-rhyme romance conventions.

extent.description: One complete verse legend. The poem division contains 107 numbered LG elements and 643 L elements. DIMEV's intrinsic description is 107 six-line aabccb stanzas; the encoding count is one line higher than 107 times six because LG 106 includes the separate Latin acclamation 'Te deum laudamus'. The six additional L elements elsewhere in the XML are editorial comparison quotations in Heuser's preface.

content_parts.label: Rawlinson tail-rhyme Theophilus poem
content_parts.summary: The complete 107-stanza Middle English legend with one embedded Latin acclamation near the close.
notes: Heuser's German preface and Bemerkungen are retained in CME00082 but are edition framing, not child parts of the medieval work.

related_works.title: The Legend of Theophilus (Northern Homily Cycle)
notes: A distinct Middle English version; do not merge its fourteen-witness Northern Homily Cycle tradition with the unique Rawlinson tail-rhyme poem.

related_works.title: Theophilus (South English Legendary version)
notes: A distinct long-line Middle English version with a multi-witness tradition.

evidence.summary: Direct evidence for artifact identifiers, German preface, poem and commentary boundaries, Middle English and editorial-language markup, headings, page milestones, stanza and line elements, embedded Latin acclamation, and revision history.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, CME00082 item record, 'Eine neue mittelenglische version der Theophilus-sage'.
evidence.summary: Official item evidence for the electronic-text title, Heuser responsibility, 1903 publication facts, public-domain assertion, and encoded preface, poem, and Bemerkungen divisions.

evidence.citation: Wilhelm Heuser, 'Eine neue mittelenglische Version der Theophilus-Sage,' Englische Studien 32 (1903): 1-23.
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the first edition, title and explicit, plot, manuscript and target locus, single-witness transcription, tail-rhyme form, fifteenth-century witness-language assessment, unresolved transmission, and stated editorial practices.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0, record 3109 (IMEV/NIMEV 1883), manuscript BodRawPoe225, and bibliography entries Heuser1903, Boyd1955, and Boyd1956.
evidence.summary: Current work-level evidence for discovery title, unique witness, ff. 11-15 locus, manuscript rubric and explicit, 107 six-line aabccb stanzas, subjects, LALME/eLALME LP 73 localization to Ely, and known editions.
notes: Record 3109's cross-reference to record 5061 is inconsistent with the release data: record 5061 is an unrelated advice poem; the corresponding South English Legendary Theophilus record is 5554.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts, MS. Rawl. poet. 225 (Summary Catalogue 14716).
evidence.summary: Official institutional evidence for the current shelfmark, paper support, English origin, 1450-1500 date, South English Legendary miscellany context, and Rawlinson provenance.

evidence.citation: Beverly Boyd, 'The Rawlinson Version of Theophilus,' Modern Language Notes 71.8 (1956): 556-559.
evidence.summary: Scholarly evidence for the established Rawlinson-version title, unique fifteenth-century copy context, distinction from two other Middle English versions, plot synopsis, Heuser's first-editor status, and the interpretation of the poem as a tail-rhyme romance rather than evidence of a direct dramatic source.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of CME00082.xml and separation of the poem DIV2 from editorial Q blocks.
evidence.summary: Confirms one DIV1, three DIV2 elements, 107 poem LG elements, 643 poem L elements, six editorial-comparison L elements, and 649 total L elements; LG 106 alone has seven L children because it includes 'Te deum laudamus'.

open_questions.question: Can the composition date be narrowed independently of the 1450-1500 date of the sole witness?
next_steps: Review post-Boyd scholarship on the Rawlinson version's language, meter, and relationship to older English and Continental Theophilus traditions without converting witness date into composition date.

open_questions.question: What earlier textual tradition, if any, directly underlies the Rawlinson tail-rhyme version?
next_steps: Review modern comparative scholarship; retain Heuser's rejection of Rutebeuf as a direct exemplar and Boyd's genre-based explanation as separate interpretations until a direct textual dependency is demonstrated.

open_questions.question: Does the manuscript rubric read 'liberatus' as Heuser prints or 'deliberatus' as DIMEV records?
next_steps: Inspect a legible image of f. 11 or request confirmation from Bodleian Special Collections; preserve both sourced readings until then.

open_questions.question: Should DIMEV record 3109's related-version pointer to record 5061 be corrected to record 5554?
next_steps: Report the release-data discrepancy to DIMEV: record 5061 is an unrelated advice poem, while record 5554 is the South English Legendary Theophilus version and reciprocally points to record 3109.

notes: The preferred English title is supplied for discovery and disambiguation; the manuscript's Latin rubric and explicit are retained separately.
The unique Rawlinson poem must not be conflated with CME00065's Northern Homily Cycle Theophilus or DIMEV 5554's South English Legendary version.
German and French in CME00082 belong to Heuser's edition layer. Latin is included as a work language only for the embedded 'Te deum laudamus' acclamation, not for the surrounding rubric alone.
The anti-Jewish stereotype subject note supports responsible discovery and contextualization of harmful narrative content.
Manuscript image availability, whole-object request routes, local caches, and reuse terms are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.
